name: Gaki
description: Gaki, also known as hungry ghosts, are creatures in Japanese Buddhism and folklore, condemned to an eternal state of insatiable hunger. They are often depicted as emaciated beings with perpetually swollen bellies and thin necks, unable to swallow food. This condition is a direct consequence of their past lives, typically marked by greed, selfishness, or cruelty. Gaki serve as a potent symbol of the karmic repercussions of negative actions and the importance of compassion and generosity. Their existence is a constant torment, fueled by an unquenchable thirst and hunger that can never be satisfied. The origins of the Gaki myth can be traced back to Indian Buddhism, where they are known as pretas, and were subsequently incorporated into East Asian Buddhist traditions. They are not merely monsters but pitiable figures, embodying the suffering caused by attachment and craving. Offerings and rituals are often performed to alleviate their suffering and guide them towards liberation.
subtitle: Hungry ghost of Japanese folklore
appearance: Emaciated figure with a swollen belly, thin neck, and small mouth. Often depicted as having long, unkempt hair and decaying skin. Their eyes are wide and filled with despair.
habitat: Desolate places, graveyards, battlefields, areas associated with death and suffering, or anywhere with concentrated negative energy.
behavior: Driven by an unending hunger, Gaki are constantly searching for sustenance, often attempting to consume anything they can find, including corpses, filth, and even human flesh. They are filled with envy and resentment towards the living, and may attempt to trick or manipulate them into providing food or succumbing to similar fates.

keyMyths.title: The Story of Maudgalyayana and His Mother
keyMyths.description: One of the most famous tales associated with Gaki involves Maudgalyayana, a disciple of the Buddha, who used his supernatural powers to search for his deceased mother. He discovered her reborn as a Gaki, suffering from extreme hunger. He attempted to feed her, but the food turned to ash in her mouth, a consequence of her past sins. This story underscores the importance of filial piety and the power of Buddhist rituals to alleviate suffering.

keyMyths.title: The Gaki Zoshi Scrolls
keyMyths.description: The Gaki Zoshi are a set of Japanese painted scrolls from the Kamakura period (1185–1333) that depict various types of Gaki and their suffering. These scrolls offer a vivid and disturbing portrayal of the consequences of greed and selfishness, serving as a visual reminder of the importance of ethical conduct. The scrolls detail the specific sins that led to their transformation into Gaki, providing a moral lesson to viewers.

keyMyths.title: Offerings for the Hungry Ghosts
keyMyths.description: During the Obon festival in Japan, offerings are made to the spirits of ancestors, including those who may have become Gaki. These offerings typically include food, water, and prayers, intended to alleviate their suffering and guide them towards a better rebirth. The practice demonstrates compassion and a belief in the interconnectedness of the living and the dead, and the importance of remembering and honoring those who have passed on.

sources.author: Tyler, Royall

sources.work: The Ghost Festival in Medieval China
sources.description: Provides context for the East Asian tradition of hungry ghost festivals, tracing the origins and development of the Gaki concept.
